risk_name: CAPEX Overrun — Total Project Cost Exceeding Budget by >20%

description.summary: Total project CAPEX exceeds the budgeted amount by >20%, eroding project returns and potentially triggering loan covenant breaches. First-of-a-kind hydrogen projects carry elevated CAPEX uncertainty due to limited reference cost data, evolving supply chains, and integration complexity.
description.detailed: CAPEX overrun sources for hydrogen projects: scope creep during FEED (additional BOP scope identified during detailed engineering), EPC contract change orders (ground conditions, interface issues), electrolyzer price escalation between bid and order (commodity-driven), construction delay costs (extended overhead, financing during construction), and contingency drawdown exceeding planned reserves. The Gold Dataset provides mixed evidence: projects with experienced developers (Air Liquide Normand'Hy, Shell HH1) have not publicly reported overruns. Masshylia's scale-down can be interpreted as pre-FID CAPEX discovery — the 120 MW project's CAPEX was likely higher than initially estimated, driving the decision to scale back.
description.root_cause: Limited reference cost data for >100 MW hydrogen projects. First-of-a-kind integration complexity. Evolving supply chain pricing. Developer inexperience in hydrogen project delivery.
trigger_events: FEED cost estimate exceeds feasibility study estimate by >15%
Contingency drawdown exceeds 50% before 50% construction completion
Major change order (>5% of EPC contract value) approved

assessment.probability_rationale: First-of-a-kind industrial projects across all sectors average 20-30% CAPEX overrun (Bent Flyvbjerg data). Hydrogen projects are no exception. Experienced developers with in-house engineering (Air Liquide, Shell, Linde) have lower probability than consortiums of smaller players.
assessment.impact_rationale: 20% CAPEX overrun on €400M project = €80M. This can push project IRR below investment hurdle rate. Lenders may require equity injection or additional collateral.

preventive_actions.description: Budget contingency at 25% during feasibility, 15% at FID, 10% at EPC contract award. Do not reduce contingency below 10% before mechanical completion.
Use competitive FEED + EPC procurement with lump-sum turnkey (LSTK) contract structure. Transfer construction cost risk to EPC contractor where possible.

monitoring_indicators.indicator: Cost performance index (CPI) = Earned Value / Actual Cost

incident_descriptions: Masshylia (GA-PR-002): The 83% scale-down (120→20 MW) can be interpreted as pre-FID CAPEX discovery — the full-scale project was not financeable at projected costs given available subsidies and offtake pricing.

Holland Hydrogen I (GA-PR-003): $1.17B (€1B) for 200 MW = €5,000/kW all-in. This is a high per-kW cost compared to IRENA benchmarks (€1,100-2,100/kW total installed) — reflecting first-of-a-kind costs including dedicated 380 kV substation, HyTransPort pipeline, and offshore wind integration.
Galp Sines (GA-PR-010): €650M total investment (combined H₂ + biofuels unit; ~€250M for H₂). EIB €430M financing provides cost credibility through lender due diligence.
evidence.lessons_learned: LSTK EPC contracts transfer construction cost risk but at a premium (10-20% higher contract price vs. cost-reimbursable). The trade-off is certainty vs. cost. Developer experience is the best predictor of CAPEX accuracy — Air Liquide and Shell have internal cost databases that new entrants lack.

sources.title: IRENA Green Hydrogen Cost Reduction 2024 — CAPEX benchmarks and sensitivity
sources.author: IRENA
